title: Friends in Disguise
summary: How microbes you can't see help your body, your food, and the planet — the beneficial-first framing of the cast.

questions.prompt: Cyanobacteria like Photo helped shape Earth's atmosphere by…
choices: Releasing oxygen during photosynthesis over billions of years
questions.explanation: Cyanobacteria use sunlight to make their own food and release oxygen — they helped build the oxygen-rich air we breathe today.

questions.prompt: Mycorrhizal fungi like Net help forests by…
choices: Linking tree roots so plants can share water and nutrients
Making leaves grow faster than is healthy
questions.explanation: Mycorrhizal fungi connect tree roots underground in a living network — many plants share resources and warnings through it.

questions.prompt: Many strains of Escherichia coli are actually beneficial because they…
choices: Help make vitamin K inside the gut
questions.explanation: Most E. coli strains are harmless gut residents — they help produce vitamin K, which the body uses for healthy bones and blood clotting.

questions.prompt: Yogurt, kefir, and kimchi all rely on which kind of microbe to ferment them?
questions.explanation: Fermented foods use beneficial bacteria (often Lactobacillus) and sometimes yeasts to transform milk, vegetables, or grain into longer-lasting, tangier foods.

questions.prompt: Akkermansia muciniphila supports your gut by…
choices: Feeding on the mucus layer and helping keep the gut wall strong
questions.explanation: Akkermansia muciniphila feeds on the gut's mucus layer — when it's thriving, the intestinal lining tends to stay strong.

questions.prompt: Deinococcus radiodurans is famous because it…
choices: Survives extreme radiation, cold, and dehydration
questions.explanation: Deinococcus radiodurans is one of Earth's toughest microbes — scientists study it to learn how life endures conditions that would defeat most cells.
